revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Bastet-Fest]][[fr:Fête de Bastet]] [[File:Louvre 042005 11.jpg|thumb|Bronze {{ill|Figurine|en|Figurine|qid=Q1066288|lt=statuette|1=Figurine}} of {{ill|Bastet|en|Bastet|qid=Q129106|lt=Bastet|1=Bastet}} in the [[Louvre]] in {{ill|Paris|en|Paris|qid=Q90|lt=Paris|1=Paris}}.]] The Bastet Festival (also Beautiful Festival of Drunkenness) was celebrated twice a year in {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}}. The "Great Bastet Festival" was first attested in the {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|de|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Old Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q177819|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} as a New Year's celebration. However, its origins trace back to the {{ill|Predynastic Egypt|de|Prädynastik (Ägypten)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Predynastic Egypt|qid=Q714601|1=Predynastic Egypt}}, during which the goddess Bastet was also venerated as {{ill|Sekhmet|en|Sekhmet|qid=Q146104|lt=Sekhmet|1=Sekhmet}}.<ref>Friedhelm Hoffmann: ''Ägypten – Kultur und Lebenswelt in griechisch-römischer Zeit – Eine Darstellung nach den demotischen Quellen.'' Berlin 2000, p. 223.</ref> The festival shared [[:en:Mythological|mythological]] connections with {{ill|Hathor|en|Hathor|qid=Q133343|lt=Hathor|1=Hathor}}, evident in the similarity of the rituals.<ref>Christian Hermann: ''Ägyptische Amulette aus Palästina/Israel: Mit einem Ausblick auf ihre Rezeption durch das Alte Testament.'' Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Göttingen 1994, pp. 145–147.</ref> Originally associated with {{ill|Upper Egypt|en|Upper Egypt|qid=Q203751|lt=Upper Egypt|1=Upper Egypt}}, its cult center later moved to {{ill|Bubastis|en|Bubastis|qid=Q603841|lt=Bubastis|1=Bubastis}}, in the {{ill|Nile Delta|en|Nile Delta|qid=Q179528|lt=Nile Delta|1=Nile Delta}}. The "Great Bastet Festival" was closely linked to the beginning of the annual {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|qid=Q1519552|lt=Nile inundation|1=Flooding of the Nile}} until the {{ill|New Kingdom of Egypt|en|New Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q180568|lt=New Kingdom of Egypt|1=New Kingdom of Egypt}}. However, due to the shifting {{ill|heliacal rising|de|heliakisch|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/heliacal rising|qid=Q368373|1=Heliacal rising}} of {{ill|Sirius|en|Sirius|qid=Q3409|lt=Sirius|1=Sirius}}, the festival was later celebrated after the arrival of the flood. The "Lesser Bastet Festival" was later held in conjunction with the {{ill|Festival of Osiris|de|Totenfest des Osiris|lt=Wag Festival|4=fr|5=Fête_Ouag|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Festival of Osiris|qid=Q3092806|1=Festival of Osiris}} (also known as the "Beautiful Festival of Drunkenness"), associated with {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|qid=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}}. == Festival Dates == The timing of the Bastet Festival was always based on the {{ill|Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|de|Sothis-Mondkalender|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar|qid=Q1944246|1=Ancient Egyptian Lunar Calendar}}, which was itself linked to the {{ill|Sothic cycle|en|Sothic cycle|qid=Q1749234|lt=Sothic cycle|1=Sothic cycle}}. The first day of the month of Thoth (1 Thoth) in the Sothic lunar calendar marked the beginning of the new year. The festival could only begin after the heliacal rising of Sirius, limiting its dates to between 1st and 29th Thoth.<ref>Rolf Krauss: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, pp. 15–17.</ref> Because of its connection to Sirius, the festival was held at a relatively stable time of year. Between the 5th millennium BCE and the time of the {{ill|Decree of Canopus|en|Decree of Canopus|qid=Q1240912|lt=Decree of Canopus|1=Decree of Canopus}}, the heliacal rising of Sirius gradually shifted from June 3 to July 14, giving a date range from June 3 to August 12. A rare celestial alignment occurred in 239 BCE (8th regnal year of {{ill|Ptolemy III Euergetes|en|Ptolemy III Euergetes|qid=Q39602|lt=Ptolemy III Euergetes|1=Ptolemy III Euergetes}}), when the Bastet Festival, the heliacal rising of Sirius, and the {{ill|Beautiful Festival of the Valley|de|Schönes Fest vom Wüstental|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Beautiful Festival of the Valley|qid=Q478205|1=Beautiful Festival of the Valley}} all coincided on the same day. To preserve this alignment, Ptolemy III issued a decree the following year introducing a leap day. == Festival Celebrations == {{ill|Herodotus|en|Herodotus|qid=Q26825|lt=Herodotus|1=Herodotus}} (Histories, II 60) provides a detailed description of the festival. Due to the Nile flood, traveling by land was difficult, so pilgrims journeyed to Bubastis by boat. Along the way, they sang loudly, danced, and shouted insults at the inhabitants of cities they passed. Women were particularly central to these rituals, often engaging in obscene banter and suggestive behavior. Upon arrival in Bubastis, the already heavy drinking intensified: *"Where is the beer? My throat is dry like straw."* Herodotus' descriptions are supported by {{ill|Demotic Egyptian language|en|Demotic Egyptian language|qid=Q36765|lt=Demotic Egyptian|1=Demotic Egyptian language}} texts, including the famous "Harpist's Song": {{Zitat|The harpist speaks to the festival attendees: ‘I cannot sing when I am hungry, nor hold my harp to sing unless I am full of wine.’ The people call out, ‘Sing!’ Once he is given wine, he begins to sing, but in his drunkenness, the harp appears upside down. When he turns the harp in his hands, he sings of ‘women’s disgrace’ (immoral behavior). |Excerpt from the "Harpist's Song" |ref=<ref>Friedhelm Hoffmann: ''Ägypten – Kultur und Lebenswelt in griechisch-römischer Zeit – Eine Darstellung nach den demotischen Quellen.'' Berlin 2000, p. 225.</ref>|qq=}} The festival mirrored the mythological behaviors of: * "Wild Sekhmet" (the chaotic and destructive goddess) * "Singing Bastet" (the joyful goddess of music) * "Dancing Hathor" (the goddess of ecstasy and festivity) == References == * {{ill|Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|de|Hans Bonnet (Ägyptologe)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hans Bonnet (Egyptologist)|qid=Q15990399|1=Hans Bonnet}}: ''Lexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte.'' Nikol, Hamburg 2005, ISBN 3-937872-08-6 (previous title: "Reallexikon der ägyptischen Religionsgeschichte"), pp. 628–629. * Christian Hermann: ''Ägyptische Amulette aus Palästina/Israel: Mit einem Ausblick auf ihre Rezeption durch das Alte Testament.'' Vandenhoeck & Ruprecht, Göttingen 1994, ISBN 3-525-53773-5. * {{ill|Friedhelm Hoffmann|de|Friedhelm Hoffmann|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Friedhelm Hoffmann|qid=Q42726857|1=Friedhelm Hoffmann}}: ''Ägypten – Kultur und Lebenswelt in griechisch-römischer Zeit – Eine Darstellung nach den demotischen Quellen.'' Akademie-Verlag, Berlin 2000, ISBN 3-05-003308-8. * {{ill|Rolf Krauss|de|Rolf Krauss|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rolf Krauss|qid=Q91239|1=Rolf Krauss}}: ''Sothis- und Monddaten: Studien zur astronomischen und technischen Chronologie Altägyptens.'' Gerstenberg, Hildesheim 1985, {{Falsche ISBN|3-8067-8086-X|qq=}}. * {{ill|Richard-Anthony Parker|de|Richard-Anthony Parker|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Richard-Anthony Parker|qid=Q2148992|1=Richard Anthony Parker}}: ''The calendars of ancient Egypt.'' Chicago Press, Chicago 1950. * {{ill|Siegfried Schott|de|Siegfried Schott|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siegfried Schott|qid=Q102228|1=Siegfried Schott}}: ''Altägyptische Festdaten.'' Verlag der Akademie der Wissenschaften und der Literatur, Mainz/Wiesbaden 1950. * M. I. Bakr, H. Brandl: ''Bubastis and the Temple of Bastet.'' In: M. I. Bakr, H. Brandl, F. Kalloniatis (eds.): ''Egyptian Antiquities from Kufur Nigm and Bubastis.'' Opaion-Verlag, Berlin 2010, ISBN 978-3-00-033509-9, p. 31. * E. Bernhauer: ''Block Statue of Nefer-ka.'' In: M. I. Bakr, H. Brandl, F. Kalloniatis (eds.): ''Egyptian Antiquities from Kufur Nigm and Bubastis.'' Opaion-Verlag, Berlin 2010, ISBN 978-3-00-033509-9, pp. 176–179. == Notes == <references /> {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar|qq=}} {{translated page|de|Bastet-Fest|version=236558773|comment=Imported full de history}}
